title: Triangulations of Surfaces
overall_summary: Hatcher proves that the arc complex A(S,V) of a compact surface with a finite vertex set is contractible except for two boundary-only exceptional families, where it is a sphere, and also proves contractibility of a larger arc-and-circle complex C(S,V) when V has an interior point. The core argument is an explicit surgery deformation: fix an essential arc β, put every arc system in minimal position with β, and successively slide intersections with β to one endpoint, discarding inessential arcs, to deformation retract the complex onto the star of β. The paper also proves that adding a boundary vertex suspends the arc complex, and uses the identification of links with arc complexes of cut-open surfaces to prove connectivity of triangulation flip graphs. For the original matching-complex problem, the most relevant material is the surgery-to-a-star paradigm, the use of weighted arc systems to make surgery continuous on cells, and link/cutting arguments for inductive connectivity or contractibility proofs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main Theorem: contractibility and exceptional spheres for arc and curve complexes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a compact connected surface, possibly with boundary, and let V be a nonempty finite subset of S. An essential arc in (S,V) is an embedded arc in S whose endpoints lie in V, whose interior is disjoint from ∂S ∪ V, whose endpoints may coincide, and which does not separate S into two components one of which is a disk meeting V only in the endpoints of the arc. An essential circle in (S,V) is an embedded circle in S disjoint from ∂S ∪ V which does not separate S into two components one of which is either a disk meeting V in at most one point or an annulus disjoint from V. A curve system is a finite collection of essential arcs and essential circles, pairwise disjoint except that arcs may share endpoints, with no two curves ambient isotopic rel V. Let C(S,V) be the simplicial complex whose k-simplices are ambient isotopy classes rel V of curve systems with k+1 curves, with faces given by subcollections, and let A(S,V) be the subcomplex spanned by arc systems only. Excluding the trivial cases where these complexes are empty: (a) A(S,V) is contractible except when S is a disk with V contained in ∂S, or S is an annulus with V contained in one component of ∂S; in these exceptional cases A(S,V) is homeomorphic to a sphere. (b) If V contains at least one point in the interior of S, then C(S,V) is contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (a), Hatcher first treats the case that each boundary component of S contains at most one point of V. Choose an essential arc β. For every simplex [α0,...,αk] of A(S,V), isotope the arc system α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk into minimal position with β. A point of the simplex is viewed as a weighted arc system P = Σ ti αi, with the arcs replaced by parallel families of total thickness ti. Near intersections with β these parallel families are pinched into a single band of total thickness. Starting at a chosen endpoint of β, cut through this band and redirect the two cut ends to that endpoint of β. In combinatorial terms, if x1,...,xm are the intersections with β ordered along β, slide x1 to the chosen endpoint to obtain a new arc system c2 from c1 = α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk, discarding arcs that become parallel duplicates or boundary-parallel; then repeat with x2, and so on. Each elementary slide takes place inside a simplex spanned by the old and new arc systems, and the motion is linear on that simplex. The resulting flow is continuous on every simplex, compatible with passage to faces, fixes the star of [β], and ends in the star of [β]. Since a star is contractible, this gives contractibility in the special case. For general boundary vertex sets in part (a), Hatcher proves separately that adding a new boundary vertex to a boundary component already containing a vertex suspends the arc complex. Iterating this suspension result reduces to the special case except for the disk and annulus families. The exceptional sphere cases arise from the base cases where A(S,V) is S^0: a disk with four boundary vertices and an annulus with two vertices in one boundary component; repeated suspensions give spheres. For part (b), the same surgery method is applied to C(S,V), choosing β to be an essential arc with both endpoints at a point of V lying in the interior of S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Surgery deformation retraction onto the star of a fixed arc
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume S is a compact connected surface and V is a finite nonempty subset with at most one point of V in each boundary component of S. Let β be an essential arc in (S,V). In the arc complex A(S,V), the star of the vertex [β] consists of all simplices represented by arc systems disjoint from β, allowing one of the arcs to be isotopic to β. Hatcher constructs a deformation retraction of A(S,V) onto this star; the deformation is a flow which is linear on the simplices through which it passes.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take a simplex [α0,...,αk] and choose representatives in minimal position with β. For a point P = Σ ti αi in the simplex, interpret the coefficients ti geometrically by replacing each αi by a thin family of nearby parallel arcs of total thickness ti, with endpoints pinched at vertices. Where the families cross β, pinch them into one band crossing β. The deformation starts at a chosen endpoint of β. It cuts partially through this band from that endpoint and redirects the two free ends of the cut band to the endpoint of β. As the cut depth increases, this removes the first intersection of the arc system with β. If x1,...,xm are the intersections in order along β, then sliding x1 to the endpoint converts the old arc system c1 into a new arc system c2, after discarding arcs that have become boundary-parallel or parallel to another arc. The union c1 ∪ c2 is again a curve system and hence spans a simplex σ1 containing the original simplex as a face; on the initial part of the deformation, points move linearly along fibers of a linear projection of σ1 to the face corresponding to c2. The procedure then repeats for x2, then x3, and so on, producing a finite sequence of simplices σ1,...,σm. At the end the resulting arc system is disjoint from β, so it lies in the star of [β]. If the original system was already disjoint from β, no intersections are present and the flow is the identity. Compatibility with faces follows because restricting the construction to a face simply deletes some weighted arcs and performs the same ordered surgeries on the remaining system. This gives a continuous global deformation retraction onto the star.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Minimal-position isotopy fact used in the surgery construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [α0,...,αk] be a simplex of A(S,V) and let β be an essential arc. Hatcher uses the standard fact that if the arc system α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk has been isotoped to intersect β minimally, equivalently so that there is no disk D in S with boundary consisting of an arc in β and an arc in some αi, with ∂D not equal to αi ∪ β and with the interior of D disjoint from V, then any two such minimal-position representatives are isotopic through a family of minimum-position curve systems.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given an isotopy F from one minimal-position representative of the arc system to another, view F as a map from the disjoint union of the αi times an interval into S. Perturb F to be transverse to β. Then F^{-1}(β) is a one-dimensional set in the source cylinder(s), and one studies the projection F^{-1}(β) → I to the isotopy parameter. By gradually simplifying F^{-1}(β) and cancelling critical points of this projection, one removes non-minimal creation or cancellation of intersections. The result is an isotopy connecting the two representatives while staying within the class of representatives having minimal intersection with β. This fact ensures that the surgery flow is well defined on isotopy classes, not on arbitrary chosen representatives.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition: adding a boundary vertex suspends the arc complex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V′ be obtained from V by adding one new vertex v′ in a component of ∂S that already contains at least one vertex of V. Then A(S,V′) is homeomorphic, in fact piecewise linearly homeomorphic, to the suspension ΣA(S,V). Here the suspension is the quotient of A(S,V) × [−1,1] obtained by collapsing A(S,V) × {−1} to one point and A(S,V) × {1} to another point.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let v be a vertex of V adjacent to the new vertex v′ on the relevant boundary component. Define two special boundary-parallel essential arcs β and β′: β joins v′ to the next vertex of V′ on the other side of v, and β′ is defined similarly with the roles of v and v′ interchanged. Let X be the subcomplex of A(S,V′) consisting of arc systems containing neither β nor β′. The full complex A(S,V′) is obtained from X by attaching the stars St(β) and St(β′) along their links, which lie in X. Hatcher embeds X into ΣA(S,V) as follows. Regard a point x of X as a weighted arc system. Collapse the boundary segment between v and v′ to identify v and v′, obtaining a weighted arc system x̄ in A(S,V). Let θ(x) be the total weight of arcs of x incident at v and θ′(x) the total weight incident at v′. Send x to (x̄, (θ(x) − θ′(x))/2) in the suspension coordinate. Since θ and θ′ lie in [0,1], the height lies in [−1/2,1/2]. For fixed collapsed weighted system x̄, the possible height values form a symmetric interval about 0 whose length is θ(x)+θ′(x), varying continuously with x̄. The map X → A(S,V), x ↦ x̄, is surjective. The complement of the image of X in the suspension consists of two open cones, corresponding exactly to the links of β and β′; their closures correspond to the stars St(β) and St(β′). Thus A(S,V′) and ΣA(S,V) are decomposed into the same three pieces, X plus the two stars/cones, with the same intersection pattern, yielding a PL homeomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Link-after-cutting identification
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ = [α0,...,αk] be a simplex of A(S,V). Let (S′,V′) be obtained from (S,V) by cutting S open along the arcs α0,...,αk, with the induced vertex set on the cut surface. Then the link of σ in A(S,V) can be identified with A(S′,V′). If S′ is disconnected, Hatcher treats A(S′,V′) as the join of the arc complexes of its connected components.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A simplex in the link of σ is represented by an arc system disjoint from α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk and not containing arcs isotopic to the αi. Cutting S open along the αi turns every such disjoint arc system into an arc system in the cut surface (S′,V′). Conversely, any essential arc system in (S′,V′) descends to an arc system in S disjoint from the αi and hence determines a simplex in the link. Disjointness, isotopy rel vertices, and passage to faces are preserved by the cutting and gluing operations, giving the simplicial identification. In the proof of the triangulation corollary, this identification is combined with the theorem: if σ has codimension at least two in A(S,V), then the corresponding A(S′,V′) has dimension at least one, hence is connected; disconnected S′ causes no difficulty because a join of the component complexes is used.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary: triangulations are connected by elementary moves
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define a triangulation of (S,V) to be a maximal simplex of A(S,V): when V meets each component of ∂S, such a maximal arc system decomposes S into triangles with vertex set V, allowing triangles with coinciding vertices or edges; in the general case, maximal arc systems decompose S into triangles together with punctured monogons corresponding to boundary components disjoint from V. An elementary move changes one edge of a triangulation in a quadrilateral or in a punctured digon, as shown in the paper. Then any two isotopy classes of triangulations of (S,V) are related by a finite sequence of elementary moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Triangulations correspond to maximal simplices of A(S,V), and all maximal simplices have the same dimension by Euler characteristic considerations. A codimension-one face is obtained by deleting one edge from a triangulation. In the two elementary-move configurations, namely a quadrilateral or a punctured digon, this codimension-one face lies in exactly two maximal simplices, corresponding to the two possible choices of the missing edge. The only other case is that the deleted edge occurs twice in the same triangle; then the codimension-one face lies in only one maximal simplex. Thus it suffices to show that any two maximal simplices can be connected by a path passing through open codimension-one faces, except for the zero-dimensional exceptional cases, which are precisely the quadrilateral and punctured-digon cases and already consist of two vertices related by one elementary move. The theorem implies that A(S,V) is connected whenever it has dimension greater than zero. Choose any polygonal path in A(S,V) between the two maximal simplices. Push the path off all simplices of codimension greater than one by induction on the minimum dimension of simplices met by the path. This pushing is possible because the link of any codimension-at-least-two simplex σ is identified with A(S′,V′) for the surface cut open along σ, and this link has dimension at least one and is connected. Hence the path can be adjusted to cross only codimension-one faces, giving the required finite sequence of elementary moves.

proof_techniques.name: Weighted arc-system surgery flow
proof_techniques.description: A point of a simplex [α0,...,αk] is represented not just formally by barycentric coordinates Σ ti αi, but geometrically as a weighted arc system: each αi is replaced by a family of nearby parallel arcs of total thickness ti. Near a fixed arc β, these parallel families are pinched into a band, and the deformation cuts through the band from one endpoint of β, redirecting cut strands to that endpoint. In combinatorial terms, intersections with β are ordered along β and removed one at a time; the transition from the old arc system to the new one lies in a simplex spanned by their union, so the deformation is linear on that simplex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the matching complex F_w, cells are already products of intervals corresponding to local crossing resolutions. This technique suggests trying to order intersections or crossings relative to a fixed reference arc/matching and define a cellular flow that resolves or slides one crossing at a time while remaining inside a cell or a controlled adjacent cell.

proof_techniques.name: Contraction onto the star of a fixed object
proof_techniques.description: Rather than proving global contractibility directly, Hatcher fixes an essential arc β and constructs a deformation retraction onto the star of the vertex [β]. The star consists of all arc systems compatible with β, meaning disjoint from β up to isotopy, and is contractible because it is a cone-like subcomplex. The whole proof is organized around making every arc system compatible with β by successive surgeries.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A parallel strategy for F_w would be to identify a canonical interval, pair, or noncrossing submatching that can serve as β, then show every matching can be flowed to the subcomplex of matchings compatible with it. If that subcomplex is a star or cone, contractibility would follow.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal-position and bigon-removal control
proof_techniques.description: Before performing surgery, arc systems are put in minimal position with respect to β. The criterion used is the absence of a disk whose boundary is an arc in β together with an arc in one of the system arcs, with interior disjoint from V. Hatcher also invokes the standard fact that any two minimal-position representatives are isotopic through minimal-position representatives, proved by studying the inverse image of β under an isotopy and cancelling critical points of its projection to the isotopy parameter.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Because F_w is built from isotopy classes of immersed intervals, any surgery or crossing-resolution operation needs to be independent of chosen representatives. A matching analogue of minimal position with respect to a reference object could make local operations well defined on isotopy classes.

proof_techniques.name: Discarding inessential or duplicate arcs during surgery
proof_techniques.description: The surgery operation can create arcs that are boundary-parallel or parallel to arcs already present. Hatcher simply discards such arcs so that the result remains an allowed arc system in A(S,V). In the special case with at most one vertex on each boundary component, he notes that not all of the weighted system is discarded.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the word-matching setting, resolving or sliding arcs may create trivial components or local cancellations. The paper’s method suggests allowing a normalization step after each local move, while proving that the hypotheses prevent the entire object from disappearing or leaving the allowed class.

proof_techniques.name: Continuity by simplex-by-simplex linearity and face compatibility
proof_techniques.description: The surgery process is shown to be continuous by decomposing each path into finitely many linear segments through simplices σ1,...,σm, where each σj is spanned by the arc systems before and after one elementary surgery. The construction respects faces because deleting some arcs from the initial weighted system commutes with the same ordered surgery procedure on the remaining arcs. These two points give a well-defined global deformation of the whole simplicial complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a prospective deformation must respect cubical attaching maps. The analogous check would be that local crossing-resolution flows are linear or product-linear on each cube and commute with passing to facets obtained by resolving crossings.

proof_techniques.name: Links as arc complexes of cut-open surfaces
proof_techniques.description: The link of a simplex represented by arcs α0,...,αk is identified with the arc complex of the surface obtained by cutting open along those arcs. This converts local combinatorics near a simplex into the same type of global object on a simpler or decomposed surface. If the cut surface is disconnected, the relevant complex is the join of the complexes of the components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Fixing part of a matching or resolving some crossings in F_w may decompose the upper half-plane or disk into independent regions with subwords on their boundaries. A link analysis modeled on Hatcher’s cutting argument could reduce local contractibility or connectivity questions to smaller matching problems and join/product decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-vertex suspension induction
proof_techniques.description: When a new vertex is inserted on a boundary component that already contains a vertex, Hatcher decomposes A(S,V′) into a subcomplex X avoiding two special arcs plus the two stars of those arcs. He embeds X into the suspension of A(S,V) by collapsing the boundary segment between the old and new vertices and recording a height equal to half the difference of the total weights incident at the two vertices. The two missing open cones in the suspension are exactly the two stars, giving a PL homeomorphism A(S,V′) ≅ ΣA(S,V).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matching complex changes predictably when adjacent boundary positions are inserted, split, or identified, this suspension-style decomposition could provide an induction on word length or on the number of boundary points.

proof_techniques.name: Pushing paths off high-codimension strata using connected links
proof_techniques.description: To prove connectivity of the triangulation flip graph, Hatcher starts with an arbitrary polygonal path in the connected arc complex and then pushes it away from simplices of codimension greater than one. The induction works because the link of each such simplex is connected, so a path entering a small neighborhood of the simplex can be rerouted through its link. This turns general connectivity of the complex into connectivity through codimension-one adjacencies.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, if one first proves the complex is connected or highly connected and can identify links of cells, this technique may help promote coarse connectivity to connectivity of a move graph or support inductive proofs of higher connectivity.

other_useful_info: Maximal simplices of A(S,V) all have the same dimension by Euler characteristic considerations. The exceptional sphere cases in the main theorem are generated from two S^0 base cases, namely a disk with four boundary vertices and an annulus with two vertices in one boundary component, by repeated use of the boundary-vertex suspension proposition. The paper notes that when V is contained in ∂S, A(S,V) is Harer’s complex AZ(Δ) from [H1]; Hatcher’s proof is an elementary alternative to Harer’s lamination-space proof. Hatcher also notes that [H2] constructs a stronger contractible cell complex whose vertices are triangulations and whose edges are elementary moves when V meets each boundary component, and cites Mosher [M] for another elementary proof of flip connectivity.
why_relevant: Contains fundamental contractibility arguments for arc and triangulation complexes of surfaces. Noncrossing matchings of boundary points are arc systems in a disk, so Hatcher-style surgery and link arguments are likely useful.
